item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This had some of the best exploration logs I've read in a while. Great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I keep forgetting to post on or upvote this one, I really like it. Love the writing and setting-building for it. +1

item.title: Get you an SCP that can do both
item.content:encoded:
When discussing my favorite SCPs with some fellow readers, or forming a quick "Top 5," I often find myself really torn between listing SCPs that I feel have the best narrative and the SCPs that feel the most authentically like database entries on a secret website about terrible things. When you look at those two aspects and you're left trying to rank things like When Day Breaks against Gate Guardian, where do you even begin? They accomplish such different things.

SCP-3333 is one of very few that makes both lists. The clinical descriptors of the area paint a vivid picture of an unknown violation of spacetime, and the logs largely avoid overwrought prose (perhaps outside of some stuff in the last log), but the "plot" remains captivating with enough twists and turns to keep you on your toes. I especially loved the play on how we still to do this day associate "up" with good and "down" with bad, and how terribly wrong we might be about that. This is my first comment on the site as a newly-registered member and I really wanted to express how well this SCP ticks multiple boxes for me. Excellent work!

item.title: Re: A strange history
item.content:encoded:
You're absolutely right, not sure how I missed that :/

And thank you for pointing out that essay!

item.title: Re: A strange history
item.content:encoded: They actually did! Right here. The author also wrote an essay about their thoughts on the article here.

item.title: A strange history
item.content:encoded:
The revision history of this article is perplexing.

From what I understand, it started with Jekeled being unhappy with his final version of the article (57). Jekeled asked djkaktus and minmin to rewrite it, which djkaktus announced prior to the rewrite. djkaktus then posted the rewrite (59) and an update comment here. The rewrite proves controversial. Several days later, minmin posts the re-rewrite (63) and an update comment here. The re-rewrite also proves controversial. A series of reverting/un-reverting the page follows, eventually leaving the minmin version up.

Then a year later, A Random Day (who is site staff) reverts the page to Jekeled's version (57) without any announcement or discussion on the page. I feel safe assuming A Random Day was authorized to make the reversion; but I find it confusing that no one involved with the article has left an explanation of this.

EDIT: As Crow-Cat has pointed out, there was indeed an announcement in the comments. Not sure how I missed that. I guess I was looking too hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the relationship is ambiguous but definitely suggests intimacy. There's no canon but headcanon, so YMMV.

The whole Dr. Williams monologue seems to be a bit of blunt exposition of the "lesson" or "moral" of 3333: that we're obsessed with cataloging the unknown, and peering into abysses, and that we can easily get tricked by an abyss that stares back, that sometimes there isn't a grand reveal. That sometimes all you realize at the top of any neverending ladder is that you've completely lost yourself in your search for the truth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you ignore everything else the rewrite works to set up (that is, it's an extrapolation of the "flaming mountain" which, in the original, is a dangling setpiece IMO), then I suppose that is the kind of ending you'll see, in which case we'll have to agree to disagree, because that's not how I recall that ending at all.

That said, I actually think I ought to restore my vote to positive, because I did enjoy this article as it first went up. Missing what I thought was a better version doesn't nullify how the original shocked me, in a vacuum.

LATE EDIT: I definitely think there may have been an issue with my past interpretation RE:the flaming mountain and its purpose. I had a chance to go through this article again and it seems clear that… Well, there is no flaming mountain; that's just what the "bodysnatchers" came up with. I can't believe it took me three years to realize that was just bait, and that Williams has a full understanding of everything — probably moreso than anyone else in the micro-setting this SCP establishes. All that said, I've changed my mind and I think I'm definitely in agreement with Lazarus Taxon above, and don't stand by my past comment.

And while I loved the fire god… I suppose it's ultimately unnecessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this, and I'm so glad the original ending has been reinstated! There's only one thing I don't understand, though…why does Dr. Williams recite that odd monologue about the God pillar and fetishes for information and all that? It just comes out of nowhere and seems out of character at best, and at worst is the last thing anybody would say in her situation.

…Also, were Annette and Dr. Williams a couple? Williams is clearly attached to Annette, but I didn't get the sense that they were girlfriends. However, on Reddit they claim that they were indeed dating. Am I missing something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Completely disagree. All that buildup for what, Williams to be attacked by a friggin Ninetails? It was extremely stupid, and like Lazarus said, it completely shattered the themes and tone the rest of the piece had conveyed. It makes the whole bodysnatchers thing completely incidental, instead of the central mystery and horror. There's no reason for this thing to be keter class if the only threat comes from being hit with a Fire Blast after climbing the thing for days and days. It doesn't make any sense at all. The OG ending was vastly superior.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As far as I know, the rewrite was rolled back after causing such backlash.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm confused.

Everyone's talking about how this article got rewritten apparently. So I looked through the previous versions in the page's history to see what the original was like. As far as I can tell though……all the previous versions seem to be the same as the current one. Am I just stupid and missing something huge? Can someone explain?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have finally gotten around to starting to comb through all of the 3000 entries. i loved this piece.

in the second exploration log, i said to myself, "that's strange", at the part where the operatives' recording devices all come on and they do roll call for the second time, but each operative gave an incorrect number. I had a feeling that something had gone wrong — which was confirmed by the last bit of log III.

I really enjoyed the way the horror gradually mounts. I have to head out for a bit for work, but I'm looking forward to checking out the original version when I get home, as judging by the comments the current version seems to be a significant rewrite.

well done. the slow burn here (pun intended!) was fantastic. an enthusiastic +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Admiral Ackbar is rolling in his grave right now. Love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was excelent… except for the "video log" described in an official document as an horror movie.
Killed the suspension of disbelief.

I think, honestly, that a fully cold clinical tone while describing the horrors would've made it even creepier. Too bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree.
The tower is just a trap, and all of its themes and mysteries are the bait: it doesn’t need to satisfy humanity’s search for a meaning, only to arouse it.
Putting a fire god at the very top of it was entirely unnecessary and, in my opinion, directly contradicted the article’s overall narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I honestly preferred the rewritten version, because the narrative decisions it made (combining the fire on the mountain with the skinwalkers, instead of making them wholly separate phenomena with little thematic connective tissue) were more cohesive than the original. As well, the ending — where Doctor Williams came face-to-face with an elder god and was completely and utterly destroyed — was really strong.

The original version is definitely good — I won't deny that. But it doesn't strike me as much as the rewritten version does. No vote for now.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Confirming this was reverted at my request, with the assent of djkaktus and minmin.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting here that SCP-3333 was reverted to revision 57 at the request of Jekeled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh gosh that was a good read. The exploration format was really creepy, made all the more impressive by how it manages to use fear of the unknown in the middle of the day in a building explicitly designed to have excellent visibility. And how the "monster" has fire and fox motif, in a firewatch tower in a forest. One of the best uses of the setting to enhance the story and atmosphere I've read on the entire site.

Also, I don't like foxes anymore. And I'm not answering my door ever again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: over and over again, never end.

A disjointing and thrilling read, happy to give this a milestone upvote.

I have to say though; this must have changed dramatically from the original. I started reading the comments and felt like I opened the wrong link, one to a completely different article. Now it feels like the equivalent of a hollywood actress who has gotten way too much plastic surgery, too soon. I guess it contextualizes some of the jarring qualities of the prose, and I fully admit that I have absolutely no clue what just happened in the story here, but it was still a great read with some wonderful moments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me too much of 87, but its not bad.

No vote for now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The rewrite killed this, it is a real shame

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yeah that's genuinely my bad, try turning it 90 degrees clockwise

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article genuinely made me feel unsettled.

Fantastic work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Original ending is still my favorite, but I respect the author's decision to ask for a rewrite and the rewrite is still enjoyable and very well done. I am confused by the final picture though??
+ spoiler i guess
- hide this content

Am I supposed to see something in it? It's labeled face.jpg but I can't tell if the dark top left thing is supposed to be a face or the owl looking thing on the right or the shoe thing on the bottom? or? I feel extremely dumb.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sure the two forest creatures will get along just fine

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm novoting the new version of this article because although the rewrite is really well done, I feel like the original horror of the article has been lost here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoting for the final pic alone

Been so long since we've had a skip with a jumpscare

item.title: Re: RE-REWRITE
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the edits, minmin. It keeps the stuff I liked about kaktus's changes and piles on more goodness. Upvote retained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to say I preferred the original too. Maybe it's just because it hasn't been polished as much, but the (re-?)rewrite is less clear to me; I found myself rereading sentences more often to figure out what they meant; but that's a minor thing.

Mostly I thought the setup of the original was terrifying and fantastic, the complete undermining of narratorial reliability that just happened to involve whole people disappearing and being mutilated, and that was fundamentally the thing that made it scary; a glitch in the world is stretched into the great unknown, and suddenly the great unknown is at your doorstep with terrifying windows into it.

Here… I dunno. Keeping upvote, because it's nice enough, but I don't see anything that particularly recommends it other than good descriptive writing and formatting. But the concept is just a firefox that wields cognitohazardous(?) effects and turns people into fire. It's not boring, necessarily, but I don't think it holds a match to the previous.

Also, minor thing I suppose, but I did like the spoiler formatting better than the separate tale formatting for the stuff at the end.

item.title: RE-REWRITE
item.content:encoded:
hi it's your favourite grass pokemon here with another authorised update

feat. new spooky images and an ending that is hopefully kind of a happy medium between the blockbuster finish and quiet creeping scare of the original

i hope y'all enjoy :x

Image credits:

c.jpg is edited by me, from this image under CC3.0 by Wikipedia user Anagoria, this image under CC0 Public Domain, and this image under CC2.0 by Flickr user Jeff Egnacz.

b-1.jpg is edited by me, from this image under CC3.0 by Panoramio user anagh, and Jekeled's original image.

face.jpg is edited by me, from an original image provided by SCP user Cyantreuse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not sure how I feel about the rewrite, it seems like it's just a bit too much now. The vagueness and the deliberate play on the need for an exploration was always clever for me, felt like an anti-087 almost.

I'm gonna have to downvote simply because I feel the change has lost a clever trope reversal and a full on duping of the Foundation in an attempt to be more "cohesive". Don't get me wrong it's still remarkably well written but it's lost what made it so special to begin with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the old version, I like the new version too.

If I had one criticism to make it that the animal fur bits during the build up seemed a tad on the nose, but my upvote remains.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thinking this over, I really like how the literal nine tailed fox is a nice metaphor for nature against man's (and the Foundation's) hubris.

I also do think tying back in the black beetle things to the smoke monster, like, bursting out of it's flaming hide, would help the piece feel stronger, for reasons that relate back to themes of the OG Jekeled version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: why not just re-edit the ending slightly if that's what most people are bothered about?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. That's why I didn't take that quote out - it's very much still there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The original version was a masterpiece, perhaps my favorite of all the 3000 contest entries. While the rewrite does preserve much of the original's quality, the ending is profoundly less satisfying. I will be keeping my upvote for the sake of the original in the edit history, but I really wish that it had been left alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh boy do I have issues with this rewrite.

The ending runs completely contrary to the SCP itself. The entire point was the slow build up to nothing , but now that's been replaced with an edgy fox demon that's so overly described it comes off as trying far too hard. It's two completely separate tones clashing in one SCP. The removal of the quote from Williams shows this change in tone the best.

"Williams: [Rapidly and quietly] There is a fetish among humans at the deepest level about enlightenment and height, about ignorance and depth. Here we are, on a castle in the sky, on a mountain in the air; the God Pillar, a recursive stack, and here at the top we find nothing, a dead world, an unfulfilled promise… [Pause] I just want—I want to go home…"

That's a brilliant line and it's a damn shame.

-1 for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But why?

The two monsters now sharing the fire lookout dimension are just ludicrously different in tone: one is a collective of sinister shapeshifters luring in teams of researchers via red herrings, the other is a Giant Spirit (Mozilla) Firefox what kills things with fire. These just don't belong in the same SCP, and the attempt to insert the latter into an existing and perfectly functional narrative - using a massive run-on description in an "audio log" which now contains very little audio - feels really awkward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm another voice that thinks the ending misses what made the OG good. It's a good SCP in its own right, but turning it from a creeping horror where what they thought was a meme was actually creatures preying on curiosity into a bombastic show of foxes, freeze-frames, and flames just feels very off. The fact that things didn't add up and didn't get perfectly tied up made sense in the original, since the indication was that 3333 was making shit up to get more people in the building to be replaced.

I'm going with a novote because, again, on a technical level it's still extremely strong and has the Kaktus seal of quality, but on a thematic level it ruins what I thought was good about the original.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ending feels overwrought compared to the original, but everything else is just as good if not better, so for me, this balances out to continue being an easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The rewrite is fun because I love a good ol' "oh shit what is that?" kaktus-style cinematic-worthy ending…

But I honestly preferred it as it was. The original wasn't about "big bad evil creatures", in fact it was almost the opposite. It played on us WANTING there to be a big bad monster. The red herrings wre what made it good (to me). The body-snatching creatures were taking advantage of our curiosity and obsession with things that sound insane (like a flaming mountain) to lure us in so they could infiltrate us.

The new ending goes against that: The flaming mountain is real and apparently there's also a nine-tailed fox randomly. Like, it's cool and all but it feels like one of those situations where a movie adapts a book, creates a good alternative ending but ultimately misses the point the original ending was meant to convey. In this case, the point to me was: Not all antagonists have to be a huge clusterfuck of mind-boggling chaos. Sometimes, they can just be simple, but clever creatures. So what if these simpler (but clever) creatures took advantage of our obsession with the spectacularly weird and wonderful?

At least, that's how I personally interpreted the original (and why I loved it).

As I said, I still really like this, but I do wish the point I felt in the original ending could be preserved somewhere. Maybe the concept can be brought over to end a future article, I don't know.

Keeping my +1 anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No problem dude. It was a blast.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I asked for this rewrite, and it fixes the big problem my original had. The original was a muddled mess of red herrings and half baked connections that never amounted to much-the mountaintop figures, the flaming mountain, the fact that the fire lookout in the picture didn't have an external walkway. This cleans up the writing, has a more on-tgeme conclusion, and is all around better. Upvoted, thanks kaktus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're right in that this is much more liberal in description, but my understanding is that the volcanism of the piece was supposed to be a central theme, and that didn't carry much in the original. This just gives that more emphasis.

+ show block
– hide block

Worth mentioning that the black parasites still exist in this version - in fact, it's no stretch to say they were in the fire watch at one point. They're the figures that the D-Class sees on the ridge, both times. The only difference is, something came in to replace them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don’t like this updated version. Initially I thought it was just because I knew the twist ahead of time and so was inured, but the ending log and the change to the creatures also rubs me wrong. The idea of these giant black parasites hiding away in nothingness and luring people into their demise was a lot spookier primarily because of how economical the writing was. In this case, you’re trying to do justice to imagery that really can’t be expressed adequately without a special effects budget, and that makes it more difficult for me to visualize and thus be spooked.

item.title: Update
item.content:encoded:
I just edited in a rewrite of this article we've been working on (we being myself, minmin, and jekeled) with help from numerous people who looked this over and gave some ideas. Honestly I loved this article beforehand, but Jekeled wanted it rewritten so now here we are. I hope this version does the original justice.

Image credit is from here.

There's more to come as soon as minmin finishes up some stuff, but we hope you enjoy this either way!

item.title: Re: A Heads Up
item.content:encoded: Yeah neither do we. The changes will alter some thematic things but all of the characters will remain in more or less the same place. Same story, moderately altered ending.

item.title: Re: A Heads Up
item.content:encoded: This is distressing. You two veterans should have more than enough oomph to get this done on your own, but definitely contact me if you want help. I love this article and don't want to see it go.

item.title: A Heads Up
item.content:encoded:
Alright so Jekeled deleted a bunch of articles the other day, and has made his intentions of pulling back from the site at large pretty clear. He approached me the other day and said he intended to delete this article as well, unless I rewrote it.

So as a heads up, so everybody knows we're on the same page, the plan is to make some changes to the themes of this article while maintaining a considerable amount of the original. This article is really good, and honestly I don't think it needs to be rewritten, but I also don't want to see it deleted. So we'll do what we need to do to keep it up, and keep Jek listed as the original author.

I've got minmin working with me on this, and we should have something ready to post here before too long. So keep an eye out for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thing i didn't like about it, as well as a few others SCP's written this way, is that it's trying too many things and not really executing any one thing perfectly.

The concept of a house that repeats itself through a trapdoor would be an awesome concept on its own, maybe each iteration would be weirder and stranger and more dangerous than the last or something. Infinite possibilities. But this isn't the point of the story at all, it just seems like a small detail. In fact, I don't think the story as a whole would be much different if the top house was simply one story above the original house. They just assess that from house 2 and up, there's no signs of life. Only 527 is different. So really, narratively, the only thing it did was increase the time it took to get from top to bottom.

The concept of birdlike beings that take over your skin is an interesting concept, although pretty tropey. But I don't really see what these beings have to do with the lookout? The same with the people at the summit. Never really touched on again. Same with the weird writings on the wall. What's up with this taking place in the future? I think this is the only SCP I've read that has done this. And what was up with all the houses at the top? And how and why did this place come into being? I'm not saying that the author should tell us the whole lore, but just throw us a few bites, since it seems so random to have a firewatch lookout house being a portal to itself, albeit at slightly higher altitude. Honestly, it just seems like a lot of horror tropes stitched together without any rhyme or reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 3796 was my favorite SCP here until I read the logs on this one, it is interesting and fun to read, it is a very good job and deserves the rating it has here

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That freaking dog tag got me +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This skip reminds me of Hellraiser, where the villain kills the heroine's father and wears his skin to trick her.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. ESP

2. Trapdoor opens up to a trapdoor in the floor of the next iteration

3. You'll notice that in Exploration four, Williams says that the D-Class never called her Doc— it was always Doctor. Doc is what the parasite called her by.

Yet the segment where the D-class sees the writing on the walls has him calling her Doc. And just before that, it was mentioned that the D-class forgot to turn-on the mic and camera. It's kinda subtle, but the implication is that the parasite has already taken over the D-class.

5. Both the writing on the walls and the "flaming mountain" stuff was the illusory carrot for the Foundation thrown out by the parasites. Purposefully vague imagery meant to imply anomalous properties that didn't exist, so the parasites could continue requisitioning personnel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a great piece, new favorite! +1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Congratulations, you invented Hive. Really. It's just Hive from the Secret Warriors comics and Agents of Shield. I know, that's just a coincidence, and that that's just my personal experience, but it's just way too similar. And even without those similarities it still feels pretty generic. A tentacle-ish monster that inhabits dead, decaying bodies from a barren parallel world? Didn't find it very scary. It was interesting in the build-up, but the pay-off was poor, especially since I was expecting something to be done with last two points below.

In addition I found several other issues:

1. How is a deaf, mute, blind person supposes to know what's around them (even though someone explained this in the comments, it should still be in the article)?

2. What's the layout of this thing? Does the trapdoor open up to the exterior stairs? The photo doesn't have any exterior walkways.

3. Why does the D-class suddenly see writing on the walls? I know stuff generally appears out of the blue in the skips that mess with your head, but there is no pay off with this. It doesn't go anywhere and the skip generally doesn't seem to be one that mentally affects people.

4. The flaming mountain. What was up with that?

It was interesting but the pay-off was poor, especially since I was expecting something to be done with above two points.

Sorry, but I'm with Darkstuff on this one. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "-And He Built a Crooked House-" meets the Roach Thralls. I like.
